speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, it doesn't happen for-- for a long period of time, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's certainly a lot warmer than it is-- than--

speaker: interviewee
text: Up north, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you're used to.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a lot of snow up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's-- I still prefer this type of weather. As long as we don't have that type of snow-- snow and ice down here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't bring your shovel down here, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or your ice pick.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm so-- I'm really glad that-- that we don't have to do this anymore. Now, if you're young, you know, you don't mind it. But when you get to my age a little bit, you-- you just prefer warmer weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December 22, 1995. The survivor is Kurt Greenhut. The interviewer is Edward Gross. The city is Boynton Beach, United States of America, state of Florida. The language is English. Tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Edward Gross, E-D-W-A-R-D G-R-O-S-S. Today, December 22, 1995, I will be interviewing survivor Kurt Greenhut, K-U-R-T G-R-E-E-N-H-U-T in Boynton Beach, Florida, USA. This interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please state and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name is K-- Kurt, K-U-R-T. And my second name is Greenhut, G-R-E-E-N-H-U-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, Mr. Greenhut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerd Grunhut, G-E-R-D G-R-U-N-H-U-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born, Mr. Greenhut?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in-- April 8, 1928, in Cologne, Germany. But my family moved down to Karlsruhe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old would that make you?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few months, that's all. Then my parents moved down to south-- southern Germany, Karlsruhe, Baden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- I would say about three months old, you know. About three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now,can you describe this town of Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's a city of about 180,000 people. It's-- it's a very beautiful city. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a Jewish community there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Approximately-- approximately 3,500 Jews lived there in that city. And I-- they-- they had a-- this facility had an opera house. They had a-- a zoo. And, you know, they-- it was a-- a typical city. Some beautiful parks, very beautiful parks. And in the beginning, you know, I-- I grew up there and I had some very good time in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe the housing facilities that you resided in when you were in Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the-- both times we lived in apartments. But we lived-- in the beginning, we lived in the Akademie Strasse, which was in a nice part of Karlsruhe. And-- but then Hitler said that we have to move into-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who resided in that household on Academy Street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father and mother, and myself. I was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there any other members of your family in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My-- both of my uncles lived in the same town. My uncle Julius and-- yeah, just-- Julius was the only one, right. And we-- we would get together very often on holidays, Jewish holidays, and it was-- as far I know, we was just like everyday living, you know, nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any cousins living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the-- Uncle Julius had several cousins-- several children. And one-- one name was Siegfried. Another was-- was called-- well, Annelise. The-- he had a whole-- a whole number of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandfather and grandmother? Were they alive at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother had passed away, but my grandfather was still living with-- with my father, my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so he's living in-- with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With you. Right, with me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon. Simon. Simon Greenhut.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, he was a-- a cigarette-- he owned a cigarette factory. Not a big one, you know, but a small one. And when Hitler came into power in 1933, he had to-- he confiscated that cigarette factory. And so my father was forced into going into some other business, and he decided that the oil and wax product business would be a good one to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between, let's say, 1934 and 1938, that's what he was in, in the-- in that type of business. But once he went into a concentration camp and came back, then the-- the whole situation changed. And prior to having this cigarette factory, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that part-- that was-- that was his job. In other words, that was his business. He didn't do anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any type of military service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He-- he fought in World War I, on both sides. Western front and Eastern front. And he was wounded several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what army did he fight for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German Army. And he-- he received an Iron Cross by the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the significance of an Iron Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, bravery and courage under fire, and-- which he did. He was really a-- from what I-- from what he told me, he had some very-- some interesting exploits. And as I said, he was wounded several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your-- any of your uncles also fight for the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- two of my uncles fought, Julius, Uncle Julius, and Uncle Albert. They both fought in the same, you know? They were three-- the three soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were your relatives, including your father, proud of their service to the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I-- I don't know too much about my relatives. But I know that my father very proud that he fought on the-- in that war. In fact, that's one reason he thought that Hitler would-- would not touch him, he as a-- as a war veteran. But it didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a-- was a-- a-- just a housewife. Housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were some of her interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was always interested in a lot of culture. She-- she liked music. She liked operas. And my father liked it, too, you know, in other words. And she was involved in-- I guess, in some Jewish activities, you know? But, of course, in--in those days, there weren't too many-- too many activities in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your education in Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my education. I went to the first grade in 1933. '34, the second grade. '35, third grade. And then we were kicked out. In other words, the German government decided that as far as Jews are concerned, it's-- Jewish boys and girls have to be taught-- have-- have to be taught in a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going to public school up to 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Three-- well, '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then for a time I went to the Jewish school. But what happened with the Jewish school is that every time you turned around, the teachers were gone because they're trying to get away and-- and-- and emigrate. So there was never a teacher around. So I would say that between 1935 and '42, I didn't get much of an education.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the feeling in the town, particularly among the Jewish community, about Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the older generation, I think they were-- you know, the older generation were OK. In other words, they-- they sympathized my-- with my parents and all that. But the younger generations, what-- that was the problem. The younger generation were indoctrinated in the-- in the-- in the schools, and indoctrinated to Nazism and whatever it stands for. And so we already see a lot of discrimination. I mean, even I, when I was-- I was in second or third grade, I already had to worry about when I leave the classroom that I wouldn't be attacked by some-- by some of these Hitler youth. So, you know, so it-- it was really-- I really was upset about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was some of your experiences with anti-Semitism after the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think-- let me put it this way, that-- what I experienced. Well, I wasn't really safe. In other words, whenever-- in other words, I walked out of my house. And let's say I would go to the synagogue. And then on the way back, some-- some of these kids would-- would attack me. And many times I was-- I was saved because my father came just in time to break it up. But I remember once that when we went into-- into the house when I thought I was safe, and I walked in-- into the house, there were already two or three kids, Nazis, that were awaiting-- bless you-- waiting for-- for-- for me. And, you know, it's-- this is the type of discrimination that happened. Or if-- if a policeman was-- was somewhere on a corner and he saw me being beaten up, you know, he would look the other way. In other words, I-- there was no police protection for me at all. I had to sort of work it on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that a lot of the Jews left the town. What was the reason for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're talking about Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the-- the point is this, that Hitler, when he got into power, it was very soon he started to, first of all, confiscate all the Jewish businesses. And-- and then all the professionals, Jewish professionals, whether it's-- whether they were teachers or professors or-- or scientists, they were-- they lost their-- they lost their jobs. They lost their jobs. In other words-- no matter how good they were. And-- and then, of course, later on we had to have a Jewish-- an ID card. And, oh, in 1935, the-- we were-- what do they call-- we were not citizen-- German citizens anymore. They took away our German citizenship. So that sort of gave us an idea-- gave a lot of people idea that it's not-- it's not a good place to stay anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my uncle-- one of my uncles moved to United States. And who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Uncle Julius. And then my Uncle Sally followed. That's two. And then my Uncle Albert moved to Argentina, so that was three. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then my-- you know, my father, of course, we got out later on, you know? But that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't your father take any steps, until, as you say, later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he felt that the-- that Hitler would not touch him as a-- as--as a war veteran. I mean, he fought almost the entire war. And that he-- and he had-- he had the Iron Cross, so he thought that he would be safe. But as far as Hitler's concerned, it's nobody safe. Not anyone that's Jewish or gypsy or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's feeling about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was, of course, a Russian national. And she-- she did pretty much what her father-- what my father did. In other words, she followed my father loyally. So whatever my father believe in, she backed him up. And-- but as-- as we found out that it made no difference at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your Jewish upbringing? How would you characterize your families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was brought up orthodox. In other words, we-- there were two synagogues in-- in Karlsruhe. One was orthodox and the other was reform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the orthodox one, if you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I can say is I think one was on Karlsruhe Strasse--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and and the other one, I don't even remember now where-- where that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one-- synagogue did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the Karlsruhe Strasse, the one that-- the orthodox. And it's a very nice synagogue. It's very-- you know, very elaborate inside with-- the-- the bima was in the middle, rather than in front. And there were balconies for the women. I mean, orthodox, that's what-- you know, what happened, you know, the separation of-- of men and women. And we had-- we had some very good times. I mean, a lot of Jewish holidays. And we were very active in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue frequently at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every week. Yeah. We went once a week. We were very-- very strict about that, yes. And my parents were very strict about it. And they-- they gave me the Jewish background, you know, as far as I learned-- you know, I learned Hebrew, for example. And in fact, when I came to United States, I think it was that background that was able to help me to-- to get a-- a-- a job as a Hebrew school teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you relate to us any of the conversations you might have heard among the Jewish community about the rise of Nazism in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think anybody was happy about it. But we-- we just-- no one knew what to-- how to deal with it. No one knew. Except for one thing, of course, that many of them decided to-- to get out. And, of course, thousands of-- of Jews-- German Jews, by the way-- did get out. Between 1933 and '38, I would-- it's close to 400,000 Jews left the United-- left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there come a time when any of your family emigrated from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, my-- my Uncle Julius, my Uncle Sally, my Uncle Albert, they left in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And members of your household--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, no. It was just three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- did your mother ever emigrate from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. In other words--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my father came back from the concentration camp, from Dachau, he realized that we better get out as fast as we could. Now, we-- he and I-- could not get out because we were German nationals, though we-- they-- they took away our citizenship, but nevertheless we're-- we're German. But my mother was a Russian. And there was a-- a treaty between-- a nonaggression pact between Germany and-- and the Soviet Union. And that period of time made it possible for anyone who's Russian to leave the country. And that's what my-- my mother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was in a concentration camp. What concentration camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was right after the-- right after Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, he was there for about three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you saw on Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- happened in November. The one that happened with us was November 10. But it happened November 9 and 10 all over Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At dawn, we would look-- see-- look out the window. We saw our synagogue burning. And-- and then we-- a few-- a few minutes later, we saw a whole mob of-- of Germans. I'm pretty sure that they-- they were prodded by-- by the Gestapo to-- to destroy all the Jewish businesses in that community. And what they did, of course, they-- they shattered the-- the-- the windows. And then they-- they took everything they could, vandalized everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, of course what happened is that we were in a Jewish community now. And we were in a Jewish ghetto, sort of, you know? So for some reason, all the men seemed to be drawn into our house. Because my-- I guess my father was a Jewish leader in our community. And so they all-- all sought refuge with him. It made no difference because they just walked up, and they just took everybody, all the men. And I was under a-- under a bed, you know? See-- I saw the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the point is I was just scared. And so I just stayed under the bed. But I knew that-- eventually I found out that it was only men that they wanted. And-- and then they-- he went-- they went downstairs, down the steps, and out in the street. And I saw them beating them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was beating who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the mob. The mob, which, as I said, was part of the Gestapo. And, I mean, my father was very lucky to-- to-- you know, be able to somehow-- being able to withstand this beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that there was a formation of a Jewish ghetto in Karlsruhe. When did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would think about 1935. In other words, after-- after that, we were-- we had a-- we were forced to move away from our area and move into the gypsy area. The gypsies had their-- their [GERMAN], or their ghetto, and we moved right next to the gypsy ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately how many Jews were in Karlsruhe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were over 3,000 Jews left in Karlsruhe. And for some reason, many of the Jews in that area did not emigrate. You know, but it just-- just one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your father after Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when he came back, he-- first of all, he got my mother out. He definitely-- you know-- and then he-- then he went to the American Consulate and-- and-- and sought to get a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to get out that way. But there was a waiting list. He was given number 29,993. So 29,993. And-- but-- and-- and waited. What else could he do? So-- but Hitler had made a decision in-- in October, 1940. He said that-- there were about 180,000 Jews left in Germany. And he said, I don't want them there anymore. I'm going-- I'm going-- I'm going out to war, you see, so I'm going-- I can't have a fifth column. Somebody-- I can't have the Jews to mess up-- mess up my--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father go to Dachau? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right-- 1938, right after-- after November 10, 1938. In fact, November 10. And they stayed there for about three months. And he told me, though, that there were-- not only-- there were Jews, but there were others as well. Dissenters, communists, or anyone who didn't like Hitler's way of governing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not permitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- would you characterize that as a defining moment for your family, that at that point they recognized that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Absolutely. I mean, after-- when he came back from Dachau, he realized that Hitler meant business. In other words, no one was safe. I mean, no one, Jews or gypsy or dissenters, communist, whatever. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so the die was cast. My mother left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your mother emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went through Sweden. In other words, she-- I-- I guess she took the train to-- to the north of Germany, and then across to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that separation when she emigrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I definitely-- I was crying and she was crying. And it was just a-- a-- a traumatic experience. Traumatic. But it had to happen. And I'll tell you the truth, it was a good thing it did happen because later on, eventually-- as we were-- we were in southern France, she was able to-- to keep in touch with us all the time, and HIAS also helped a great deal. HIAS, H-I-A-S, Hebrew Immigration, something. They-- they were very active in--in trying to save Jew-- Jewish people, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your mother went to Sweden but what was her final destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York. When she got to New York, she decided to get away-- to-- to go to Boston because, I-- I guess-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't really-- I really don't know exactly why. She just said-- she just decided to go to Boston, and that's where she found a job as a domestic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your-- where did your father go at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your-- your-- you and your father were then left in Karlsruhe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh,we were alone. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what happened is that once-- once he came back, he had nothing to do but work for the government. In other words, the state. And the state had plenty of things for him to do. And I understand he got very little pay for it. But what is he going to do with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he decided to-- he sent me to Heidelberg to foster parents. They became my foster parents. They were called Wierheimers. Their name was Weirheimer. And, by the way, they never made it after the-- in other words, they-- they just never made it. They ended up in, I guess, in Auschwitz or Theresienstadt. How did your father get in contact with these foster parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he would-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he would contact them probably through-- through the phone. I mean, they had phones already in those days. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's all. Oh, he-- he visited me. Visited me several times, you know. In other words, on occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did he feel a need to send you to Heidelberg to live with these foster parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what's he going to do with me? In other words, he-- he's doing some heavy labor, constructing highways or whatever. And-- and-- and what's he going to do with me? There's no school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they found someone, a family in Heidelberg, that are willing to--to take me. But my-- my-- my experience in Heidelberg wasn't very pleasant because they already have a lot of these Nazi-- Hitler youths around. And when we used to go to the mountains, you know, you cross the river and went to the mountains, there are always some people that--that would know-- recognize us, and would chase us. I've learned how to run very, very well. That's the only way I can get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in-- right in the main street, in the main street, they-- they were able to corner me, and were going to put-- get me into a garage and were going to beat me up. But for some reason, I just got away from them and ran right down the street, you know? And-- but that's the type of situation that I-- I faced. Did you have any identifying markers as a Jew at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Heidelberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's see. In 1930-- about one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with your father during that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we corresponded by letters and by him visiting me. And-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother? Did you have any contact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, definitely. He heard from-- and my mother, of course, was very concerned about what was going on in Germany. And she kept-- she kept sending us money and-- and so on. You know, in other words-- and by the way, the-- the important thing is that that money really came. In other words, it-- there were middle-- middle men there. But it made no difference, and it got there. Got to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides letters, did you have any physical contact with your father during that one year you were in Heidelberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, several times. Yeah. Physical-- by physical, you mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I saw him, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other relatives that you saw while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
